drafts.title: 18 summers (Take + Texture hybrid — origin story shape)
drafts.hook: Someone told me once you only get eighteen summers with your kid.
drafts.caption: this is my why
drafts.body_markdown:
# 18 summers

The origin story of the concept that sits behind the whole structured-freedom thesis. (Raw source: [[voicememo-2026-05-20-18-summers]].) Someone said it in a business conversation; Dan asked what they meant; it stuck. The sticky note on the wall is the load-bearing visual. The close names Rowie and the seventeen left.

Earlier draft (trimmed reminder shape, no resolution) preserved in version history. Iteration: that version cut too much; the story of where the concept came from is the video, and the business-context framing earns the "build something that doesn't need me at my desk" line later in the script.

## Script

**Hook (0-5s)**, talking head, V1/V3 visual signature:

> "Someone told me once you only get eighteen summers with your kid."

**Beat 1 (5-22s)**, talking head:

> "I asked what he meant. He said: life moves fast, and it's easy to miss more than you intend. You'll be lucky to get eighteen, so make them count."

**Beat 2 (22-40s)**, talking head, with B-roll cut to the sticky note on the wall on the "sticky note on my wall" line (2-3s hold, then back to talking head):

> "We were talking about career plans. About going after the thing you want. But the line stuck. I've had it as a sticky note on my wall ever since."

**Beat 3 (40-55s)**, talking head. This is the last spoken line of the video:

> "A little reminder of why I'm trying to build something bigger than me from this desk. And a reminder to know when to leave it."

**Close (55-62s)**, cut to Rowie for the final 5-7s hold. No VO. The video ends on the visual, not the maths. The hook already established the count; restating it at the end was redundant.

**Word count:** ~95 words. ~55-65s at conversational pace.

## Production notes

- **Same setup as V1/V3.** Shirt, chair, window. This is the third pinned-quality video; visual consistency is doing real work by now.
- **Two B-roll inserts.** The sticky note (mid-video) and Rowie (close). Everything else is talking head. Don't overload it.
  - **Sticky note shot:** the actual note on the wall. Frame it tight. Dan's handwriting if visible. Slight depth-of-field if the Ricoh allows. 2-3s hold. This is the only mid-video visual cut and it's the central image.
  - **Rowie shot:** single hold, 4-5s, after the last spoken line. Back walking, hand in Hannah's, or quiet play. Don't shoot to camera; shoot her doing her own thing. Slightly out of focus is fine. Hold past the point where you'd normally cut.
- **Delivery — the load-bearing beats:**
  1. **The hook.** "Someone told me once..." — say it like you're starting a story at the pub, not announcing a thesis. Eye contact, plain.
  2. **The quoted line.** "Life moves fast, and it's easy to miss more than you intend. You'll be lucky to get eighteen, so make them count." — this is the quote, treat it as one. Don't perform it. Just relay it the way the person said it to you.
  3. **"But the line stuck."** Said quietly. The pivot from career-context to personal-meaning lives here.
  4. **The closing beat.** "Build something bigger than me from this desk. And a reminder to know when to leave it." — the two sentences do different work. The first names the ambition, the second names the discipline. Slight pause between them so "and a reminder to know when to leave it" lands as a separate idea. This is the last spoken line of the video. Let it sit. Then cut.
- **Pace.** Conversational. Slight pauses between beats. The video earns its 65 seconds by giving each beat a beat. Don't rush.
- **Music.** Low and ambient, or off entirely. This is closer to a Take than a Texture; the voice carries the story and music can compete. If music is in, it should sit well under the spoken line and never swell. Pick at edit. The earlier poetic format wanted music underneath; this iteration is less reliant on it.
- **Captions** on for every spoken line. Same font and position as V1/V3.
- **No end card, no logo, no follow prompt.** Last hold on Rowie carries out. Cut.

## What's locked vs what to figure out on shoot

**Locked:**
- The four spoken beats and the close.
- The sticky note as the single mid-video B-roll insert.
- The single-hold Rowie close.
- "Rowie's already had one. Seventeen left." — keep the bluntness, don't soften.

**Figure out on shoot:**
- The exact framing of the sticky note shot.
- Which Rowie shot to use for the close (fresh capture today, or strongest still from archive).
- Whether music is in or out — try the edit both ways, pick the version that lands quieter.
- Whether to trim one of the middle beats if it runs long. Beat 2 ("We were talking about business. About going after the thing you want.") is the most cuttable; it can compress to "It came up in a business conversation. But the line stuck." if the video needs to come in under 60s.

## Caption (TikTok post)

**Pick:**

> this is my why

Lowercase intentional (TikTok register). Three words, ambiguous, the viewer has to watch to find out what "my why" is. Pairs cleanly with the visual close (Rowie hold) — the caption labels the why, the video shows what the why looks like. The video resists labelling its meaning; the caption does the labelling work the video chose not to. That's the division of labour.

**Alternatives if "this is my why" reads too declarative on the day:**

- *On a sticky note on my wall.* — visual hook, makes you watch to find out what's on the note
- *A little reminder.* — most oblique, pairs with the sticky note B-roll
- *Eighteen summers.* — most compressed, leans on the concept doing its own work
- *Someone told me once you only get eighteen summers.* — pulls the hook into the caption; risks giving away the opener

## Hashtags

Use the locked **Dad pillar** set from [[tiktok-voice-hooks]]:

`#dadlife` `#fatherhood` `#firsttimedad` `#workingdad` `#newcastle`

The video sits in Dad pillar even though Beat 3 carries Founder content — the emotional through-line is Dad, so use the Dad set and let the on-screen targeting text overlays carry the Founder/founder-economy signal. Don't mix in Founder hashtags; it dilutes the audience signal.

If `#newcastle` skews UK in your geo, swap to `#newcastlensw`.

## On-screen targeting text (algorithmic signal)

TikTok's OCR reads text overlays and uses them for audience targeting. Drop 3-5 of these on screen briefly (1-2 frames), then drag off-screen so they don't compete visually with the content. The algorithm sees them; the viewer barely does.

Don't stack more than 5 — the signal gets noisy. Pick the ones that match the audience you want this specific video to reach, not every audience you'd ever want.

Recommended set for this video (audience: dads building something on the side, in their 30s, thinking about presence vs ambition):

- `for dads building something on the side`
- `young dad founder`
- `Australian dad creator`
- `structured freedom`
- `first time dad`

Alternative set (broader / softer / parenting-led):

- `for parents thinking about time with their kids`
- `dad work life balance`
- `18 summers`
- `Newcastle Australia`
- `fatherhood and ambition`

Niche set (founder / indie / creator economy lean):

- `indie founder dad`
- `building while raising kids`
- `creator economy dads`
- `solopreneur dad`
- `decoupling time from income`

Mix and match. Caveat: this technique works because TikTok OCRs text in video frames, but hashtags and caption are still the more reliable signal. Don't over-engineer it. Pick a clean set, drop them in CapCut, drag off-screen, move on.

## Pre-shoot checklist

- [ ] Shoot the talking head — same setup as V1/V3
- [ ] Capture the sticky note (one cut, tight frame, 2-3s hold)
- [ ] Capture or pick the closing Rowie shot
- [ ] Record alt takes of the hook and the close (these carry the most weight)
- [ ] Decide on music at edit
- [ ] CapCut edit, ~60-75 min

## Related

- [[tiktok-plan]] (Texture format — origin story variant pushes this closer to a Take with Texture inserts)
- [[02-bench]] (other concepts: decoupling, the 5:25am case, the bits no one writes down, the Newcastle case, the bet)
- [[04-self-intro]] (complementary — V3 says who you are, this says why)
- [[01-decision-paralysis]] (similar admission-shape Take)
- [[tov]]
- [[personal-brand]]

drafts.title: Why I wear white (story-led Take — uni-rule origin, design principle implied)
drafts.close: Years later, it's a visual signature. I love the simplicity of it.
drafts.caption: a rule I made in uni and never stopped
drafts.body_markdown:
# Why I wear white

Two versions of the same concept. The short version is what gets shot for TikTok. The long version is a ramble essay that gives the idea proper room — usable as a LinkedIn long-form post, a Substack piece, a newsletter, or a TikTok long cut for the followers who want depth.

Origin in uni — Dan made a rule for himself around 2018 to only buy light-coloured clothes, after noticing most designers around him were defaulting to black. The rule became a visual signature. Both versions let the design principle (boundaries make better design) surface through the story rather than naming it as a thesis.

The short version follows a variant of the story-led reflective Take shape in [[tiktok-voice-hooks]]. Difference from 18 summers: no external quote, no "someone told me once." This is a self-made rule, not an inherited line.

Per the short + long version pattern in [[tiktok-voice-hooks]], the long version is drafted first as a ramble, then cut down to the TikTok short. The two openers differ deliberately — short hits with a declarative scroll-stopper, long starts in the story.

---

## Short version (TikTok cut)

What gets shot, captioned, hashtagged, scheduled.

### Hook (three options to pick from at shoot)

**Pick (recommended):** *"I only wear white clothes."*

On-voice. Declarative scroll-stopper without tipping into advisory register. Signals the right audience (designers + creators) and matches the body, which reflects rather than advises.

**Variant:** *"Why you should only wear white clothes."*

Louder TikTok-native shape. Higher view ceiling because it triggers the "wait, why?" curiosity, but the advisory framing doesn't match the personal body. If you use this, the viewer comes in expecting prescription and gets a story — a soft-bait move that can work, but it's a sharper craft choice than a clean voice match. Test if you want to see the algorithm payoff. Risk: signals a more general lifestyle-advice audience than the page is built for.

**Variant:** *"My wardrobe is mostly white. It started as a design experiment."*

The original. More reflective, narrative-opening. Pairs well with the long version's opener. Use if the declarative feels too punchy on delivery.

### Script (with locked hook)

**Hook (0-5s)**, talking head, V1/V3 visual signature, Dan in white (the proof is on camera):

> "I only wear white clothes."

**Beat 1 (5-20s)**, talking head:

> "I made the rule in uni, around 2018. Most designers I knew were wearing black. Like a uniform. I wanted to work out my own style without defaulting to it."

**Beat 2 (20-45s)**, talking head with B-roll cut on the "texture, layering, silhouette" line. One close-up shot (linen catching window light, ribbed knit, layered shirt under jumper) for 2-3s, then back to talking head:

> "Once colour was off the table, two things changed. Mornings got quieter. Fewer decisions before breakfast. And I started paying attention to texture, layering, silhouette. The bits that do the work when you take colour out."

**Beat 3 (45-58s)**, talking head:

> "Good design lives inside boundaries. I'd known that for years. Just hadn't applied it to me."

**Close (58-72s)**, talking head. No visual cut on the close — the wardrobe and texture B-roll have already done the visual work mid-video:

> "Years later, it's a visual signature. I love the simplicity of it."

**Word count:** ~130 words. ~70-80s at conversational pace.

---

## Long version (ramble essay)

For LinkedIn long-form, Substack, a newsletter, or a TikTok long cut (2-3 min) for the followers who want depth. Prose, not script-formatted. Less structured, more flow. The same concept given room to wander.

### Essay

> I made the rule for myself in uni, around 2018. I'd only buy light-coloured clothes. Mostly white.
>
> It came out of noticing that most designers I knew were wearing black. Black t-shirts, black jeans, black trainers, black tote. Like a uniform. Black wasn't a decision they'd all made. It was the absence of one. The default settled in and nobody chose against it.
>
> I wanted to figure out my own style without defaulting. So I made the opposite rule and held myself to it.
>
> A few things changed once colour was off the table.
>
> Mornings got quieter. Fewer decisions before breakfast. Pick a shirt, pick a layer, walk out. There's a known thing about decision fatigue. The people writing about it have a point. Strip a layer of choice out of the day and you keep some attention for later.
>
> The other change. Without colour, texture and layering and silhouette had to do the work. A white linen shirt over a white cotton tee reads completely different to a white knit over a white twill. The information that was previously in colour moved into material, weight, drape. I started caring about that stuff. The way I shopped turned into a craft question.
>
> Years later, the rule's still on. I don't think about it anymore. I buy white things, or near-white things, when I need clothes. The rule did its job. It became a signature, then it became invisible, then it became how I dress.
>
> The principle behind it is the same principle behind every brand system I've ever worked on. Tight palette, tight type, tight grid. The boundary is what makes the work get interesting. I'd applied that principle to brand work for years before I noticed I could apply it to me.
>
> What I love most is the easier mornings. The design principle is real and the signature is real, but the simplicity is what I notice every day. One less decision. Less is the gift.

**Word count:** ~320 words. ~2-3 min spoken, comfortable LinkedIn / Substack read.

### Where this version goes

- **LinkedIn long-form post.** Lift directly. Lead image: a Ricoh still of the wardrobe or a layered white outfit.
- **Substack / newsletter.** Same, possibly with one or two more paragraphs of texture (a specific outfit, a moment a stranger commented on the rule, the one near-breakage).
- **TikTok long cut (2-3 min).** Reads the essay over a slower montage of wardrobe / texture / desk / Newcastle stills. Will get less algorithm push than the short, but the followers who care about depth will watch.
- **Speaking it out loud.** If the concept comes up in a conversation or interview, the essay is the structure to talk from.

---

## Production notes

- **Same setup as V1/V3/18-summers.** Shirt, chair, window. Dan wearing white is the visual proof — the wardrobe is right there. Lock the same visual signature.
- **One B-roll insert, mid Beat 2.** The texture/layering close-up. One tight shot — a piece of linen catching window light, the texture of a ribbed knit, or a layered shirt under a jumper. 2-3s hold. Pick the one with the strongest light and contrast. If you want a faster cut sequence (texture → layer → silhouette), keep each beat under 1s and don't exceed 3s total. This is the only B-roll; the rest is talking head. The personal story does the structural work.
- **Optional wardrobe-establishing shot, Beat 1.** A tight frame of a rack of white shirts, or a stack of folded light-coloured tops. Adds a visual anchor for "I'd only buy light-coloured clothes." Skip if friction; Dan-on-camera-in-white is doing the proof work anyway.
- **Delivery beats:**
  1. **The hook.** "I made a rule for myself in uni. I'd only buy light-coloured clothes. Mostly white." — three short statements building the rule. "Mostly white" lands as a small honest concession, not a slogan. Say it like you're explaining a thing about yourself, not selling a principle.
  2. **"Like a uniform."** Drop it as a small aside. Don't underline it; the comparison does its own work.
  3. **Beat 2's texture list.** "Texture, layering, silhouette." Don't rush. Each word gets its beat. The kicker — "the bits that do the work when you take colour out" — said plain, no rhetorical underline.
  4. **Beat 3 as a quiet realisation.** "I'd known that for years. Just hadn't applied it to me." — slightly self-effacing register. Don't perform the principle; let the self-effacement carry it.
  5. **The close.** "Years later, it's a visual signature. I love the simplicity of it." — the "I love the simplicity of it" is the warm landing. Said plain, as a genuine admission, not a performance. The warmth is earned by the rest of the script being dry — it lands because nothing else in the video pitched the rule.
- **Pace.** Conversational. The video's longer than 18-summers and needs a bit more breathing room. Small pauses after the hook, after the texture list, before the close. Don't rush through the uni-context beat — that's where the audience locks in.
- **Music.** Off, or very light ambient. This is a Take — voice carries. No swell, no bed that competes.
- **Captions** on every spoken line. Same font and position as V1/V3/18-summers.
- **No end card, no logo, no follow prompt.** Close lands and cut.

## What's locked vs what to figure out on shoot

**Locked:**
- The uni-rule origin as the opener.
- The four-beat structure (rule → context → outcome → realisation) + close.
- "I love the simplicity of it" as the closing register (warmth earned by the rest of the script staying dry).
- The texture/layering B-roll as the central visual evidence.

**Figure out on shoot:**
- Which specific texture/layering shot to pull. Linen probably reads strongest on camera; ribbed knit second; layered shirt-under-jumper third.
- Whether to add a wardrobe-establishing shot (the rack of white shirts, or folded stack) earlier in Beat 1, or rely on Dan-on-camera-in-white as the wardrobe proof. The latter is cleaner; the former is more illustrative.
- Whether to compress the hook to two lines if it runs long: "I made a rule in uni. Only light-coloured clothes, mostly white." Saves 2-3 seconds. Use only if the video overruns.
- Whether "Like a uniform" stays in or comes out. It's the strongest aside but also the most stylistic — cut if delivery doesn't land it as throwaway.

## Caption (TikTok post)

**Pick:**

> a rule I made in uni and never stopped

Lowercase. Story-implying. Doesn't say what the rule is — the curiosity gap is the whole hook of the caption. Pairs with the video's story-led shape.

**Alternatives:**

- *wearing white wasn't an accident* — playful, on-voice, ambiguous
- *the cleanest rule I've kept* — leads with the rule, slightly aphoristic
- *started in 2018, still going* — minimal, date-anchored, makes you watch
- *constraints, but for clothes* — original draft caption; still works if you want the principle in front

## Hashtags

Use the locked **Tastemaker pillar** set from [[tiktok-voice-hooks]]:

`#design` `#brandidentity` `#designinspiration` `#designtaste` `#designprinciples`

The fifth slot is the subject flex — `#designprinciples` fits this video because the argument is the principle itself. Variants if the subject leans more toward the wardrobe than the principle: swap `#designprinciples` for `#minimalism` or `#wardrobedesign` to pull in the adjacent fashion-minimalism audience. The base four hold.

## On-screen targeting text (algorithmic signal)

Five OCR overlays, dropped briefly during the video then dragged off-screen. Audience signal for the Tastemaker / Design audience, with a few personal-style tags layered in:

- `Australian designer`
- `design taste`
- `personal style designer`
- `for designers thinking about style`
- `wearing white designer`

Same mechanic as [[05-18-summers]] — 1-2 frames visible, dragged off-screen so OCR catches them but viewers don't. Don't stack more than five.

## Pre-shoot checklist

- [ ] Shoot the talking head — same setup as V1/V3/18-summers, Dan in white
- [ ] Capture the texture/layering close-up (one tight shot — linen, knit, or layered fabric)
- [ ] Decide whether to add a wardrobe-rack establishing shot (skip if friction)
- [ ] Record alt takes of the hook (the "in uni" framing has to land plain) and the close (the "I love the simplicity of it" line has to read as genuine, not performative)
- [ ] CapCut edit, ~45-60 min

## Related

- [[tiktok-plan]] (Take format)
- [[02-bench]]
- [[tiktok-voice-hooks]] (Tastemaker pillar, Design / craft pillar, hashtag set)
- [[05-18-summers]] (different shape — story-led reflective Take — but the close-bookend pattern is similar)
- [[01-decision-paralysis]] (other Take exemplar)
- [[tov]]
- [[personal-brand]]

drafts.title: V3 — The Take, How I got here (evolved with stakes close)
drafts.hook: I'm a designer in Newcastle. One kid, second on the way. Here's how I got here, and what I'm trying to build now.
drafts.close: Trying to build something that earns while I'm at bedtime. Hardest thing I've tried. Posting the work here so I keep going.
drafts.caption: Sixty-second version of how I got here. The plan now is to build products that compound so I'm not trading time for money for the next thirty years. Posting the work here so I keep going.
drafts.body_markdown:
# V3 — The Take: How I got here (evolved)

Evolution of the lean arc in [[tiktok-plan]]. Same 10-chapter body. Evolved hook and close to name the stakes and the bet. Forward-facing landing where the original ended on "Pick a chapter."

## Why this evolution

The original V3 reads as biography. The 19 May info dump (silly-on-camera, the terror, decouple time from value) wanted to land somewhere in the seed batch. Two paths: write a fresh "why I'm doing this" video, or evolve V3 to do both jobs. Picked the evolution because biography + stakes is more interesting than either alone, and it keeps the seed batch shape intact.

Trade-off accepted: tonal shift in the last 10 seconds. Mitigated by keeping the body rhythmic and the close plain. No buildup, no clever flip.

---

## Script

**Hook (0-3s)**, talking head, chest-up:

> "I'm a designer in Newcastle. One kid, second on the way. Here's how I got here, and what I'm trying to build now."

**Beats** (each ~4-5s, hard cut between, text overlay = chapter title):

1. *Newcastle*
   > Grew up here. Dad's Aussie, mum's Malaysian. Third of four kids.

2. *Netherlands*
   > Moved to the Netherlands at fourteen. Skated for two years. Design clicked for me there.

3. *Back home*
   > Came back. Finished school on my own at sixteen. Spent a gap year building a motorbike.

4. *Melbourne*
   > Moved to Melbourne. Studied communication design at RMIT.

5. *STREAT*
   > First job: graphic design at STREAT. Social enterprise.

6. *Compassion*
   > Moved back to Newcastle for a job at Compassion. Started as a graphic designer. Became a field photographer. Took photos of extreme poverty.

7. *Borne*
   > Quit. Got into freelance. Started a t-shirt company with mates.

8. *On the road*
   > Six months around Australia during COVID with Hannah and my best mate.

9. *Mudbath*
   > Came back. Took a UX job at Mudbath. Reskilled.

10. *Now*
    > Senior UX designer at Endava. Building Lia on the side. Married Hannah. One kid, second on the way.

**Close (55-70s)**, back to talking head, plain:

> "Trying to build something that earns while I'm at bedtime. Hardest thing I've tried. Posting the work here so I keep going."

**Word count**: ~175 words. Target 65-75s at TikTok pace.

---

## Production notes

- Same setup as V1. Lock visual signature.
- Two head positions or two angles. Hard cut between every beat.
- Text overlay per chapter. Same font and position every time.
- No music. Voice carries.
- Beat 6 (Compassion field photography) is the heaviest. Say it plainly and move. Don't dwell. The dedicated video on that chapter is slotted for video 8-12 per [[02-bench]].
- The close lands on "Hardest thing I've tried." Say it without weight. Plain admission, not confessional.
- Optional: one Ricoh still on the close (Newcastle morning, the desk, Rowie's bath, the bike) if it adds rather than distracts.

## Caption (TikTok post)

Draft:

> Sixty-second version of how I got here. The plan now is to build products that compound so I'm not trading time for money for the next thirty years. Posting the work here so I keep going.

## Hashtags

`#ux` `#uxdesign` `#designsystems` `#aiindesign` `#dadlife` `#newcastle`

4-6 max.

---

## Source material

- [[tiktok-plan]] V3 lean arc (the original draft)
- [[dan-life-arc]] (biographical source)
- [[profile]] (stagnation-not-failure framing)
- [[goals_and_values]] (creative courage gap, structured freedom)
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (the info dump that triggered the evolution)

## Pre-shoot checklist

- [ ] Lock the shirt + chair + window (use the same as V1)
- [ ] Print or memorise the 10 chapter beats so each is one-take
- [ ] Decide whether the close is single-shot or has one Ricoh still
- [ ] Confirm caption + hashtags
- [ ] Handle and bio-line live before scheduling

## Related

- [[tiktok-plan]] (Spine)
- [[01-decision-paralysis]] (Companion video, opens the page)
- [[03-queue]]
- [[tiktok-voice-hooks]] (Voice rules informing this script)
- [[tov]]
- [[personal-brand]]

drafts.title: V3 — Lead UX self-intro (locked)
drafts.hook: How do you build something new without losing time with your family?
drafts.close: I've never really made TikToks before, so follow along if you want to watch me figure this out.
drafts.caption: Lead UX designer + dad + side-building a company that earns when I'm not in a meeting. Posting the work so I keep going.
drafts.body_markdown:
# V3 — Lead UX self-intro

Pinned self-intro video. Replaces the original chronological-CV V3 (preserved at [[03-how-i-got-here]]). Built off the research in [[tiktok-intro-script-patterns]] and the audience definition in [[tiktok-audience]].

Iteration history (2026-05-19, full day):
- Chip-stack version → too braggy, stop-start
- Conversational-prose version with bet + irony + team → closer but corporate-ish in places
- Snappy manifesto cut → genuinely too short, lost the texture, "not me" per Dan
- Longer return with biographical arc + dual anxiety + Compassion → almost there but smug hook ("made for this moment")
- **Locked version** (this one): viewer-question hook ("How do you build something new without losing time with your family?"), flowing prose, Compassion named, dropped "two friends" (solo framing), dropped dual-anxiety bullets in favor of the integrated "best of me" line.

Voice signature: Dan-confirmed as his tone. "This is my tone of voice. I'm really happy with this."

## Why this shape

The original V3 read as Wikipedia: 10 chronological location/job beats with no narrative pull. Research showed (a) zero follow-tier creators use that format, (b) the working pattern is a viewer-identification hook + a compressed personal arc + a felt-tension/anxiety beat + a forward-looking activity + a vulnerable close.

The locked shape:
1. **Viewer-question hook** ("How do you build something new without losing time with your family?") — names the exact tension the rest of the script answers. Reads as a peer asking the same question, not an expert offering wisdom. Sets up "follow along if you want to watch me figure this out" as a perfect close.
2. **Identity + arc** (one flowing paragraph) — half-Malaysian, half-Australian + Lead UX + Newcastle + global tech agency. Then the compressed 3-beat arc: traditional graphic design → humanitarian photographer (Compassion) → UX reskill. Then current: lead by day, studio on side, Borne built-and-sold. All in one paragraph that reads like Dan introducing himself at a dinner party.
3. **Crossroads + dual feeling** — names the crossroads, names family priority + September deadline, names the AI moment urgency, names the career skills, names the FOMO anxiety, names the family-cost concern. Final line ("family having the best of me") is the script's emotional pivot.
4. **Activity + documenting** — what Dan is actually doing (building creative products and workflows alongside the rest of life, documenting the process).
5. **Vulnerability close** — "never really made TikToks before, follow along."

No childhood. No 10-beat geography. No LinkedIn bullets. The deeper personal arc (road trip, Netherlands, the motorbike year, the full Compassion field-photography chapter, Borne in detail, named individual products like Lia/NOBONK/Voxquote/OpenScripture) becomes individual dedicated videos later — slotted per [[02-bench]].

---

## Script

LOCKED 2026-05-19. Conversational pace, flowing prose. Mostly talking head with optional light B-roll. Hard cuts at paragraph boundaries, not within paragraphs.

**Hook (0-5s)**, talking head, chest-up, plain, locked V1 visual signature:

> "How do you build something new without losing time with your family?"

**Identity + arc (5-30s)**, talking head, optional brief B-roll on the "Compassion" mention (a single still from the field-photography era if available, or stay on Dan):

> "Hi, I'm Dan. I'm half Malaysian, half Australian, and I'm a Lead UX designer in Newcastle working at a global tech agency. I started in traditional graphic design and had a stint as a humanitarian photographer for Compassion before reskilling into UX. Now I lead design teams by day and run my own studio on the side. I've even built and sold a small clothing company called Borne along the way."

**Crossroads + felt tension (30-55s)**, talking head, plain. Land "family having the best of me" with the most weight — slight pause before it if it feels right on camera:

> "Right now I feel like I'm at a crossroads. Family is my top priority, and we have a second kid due in September. But the AI world is moving so fast, and I've spent my career building the exact set of skills I can use to make something of this moment. If I don't build now, I feel like I'll get left behind. But I don't want to do that at the cost of my family having the best of me."

**Activity (55-65s)**, talking head, plain. Optional B-roll over "creative products and workflows" — a side-project screen or Claude window:

> "That's why I'm spending the next few months building creative products and workflows alongside the rest of life. I'm going to document the whole process."

**Close (65-72s)**, back to talking head, plain. No B-roll. Say it and stop:

> "I've never really made TikToks before, so follow along if you want to watch me figure this out."

Delivery note: Dan delivers Activity + Close as one continuous breath on camera (single paragraph in spoken flow). The split here is structural for the script doc + bundle parser, not a pause-cue.

**Word count**: ~215 words. ~65-72s at conversational pace.

---

## Production notes

- Same visual signature as V1. Lock the shirt + chair + window. Treat this as the second pinned video; visual consistency with V1 establishes the page identity.
- Mostly talking head, conversational pace (not staccato). Hard cuts at paragraph boundaries, not within paragraphs.
- **Delivery beats** for the locked script:
  - Hook is the scroll-stopper — say it like you're genuinely asking, not announcing. Eye contact matters.
  - Identity + arc paragraph runs as one flowing breath (it's long but it should feel like Dan introducing himself at a dinner party, not a CV recital).
  - "Family having the best of me" is the script's emotional pivot. Land it with a slight pause before, no pause after. Don't milk it; let it sit.
  - Close should sound mildly self-deprecating but warm — "I've never really made TikToks before" is the honesty that earns the follow.
- **B-roll opportunities** (light, not required for the script to land — voice carries):
  - Identity paragraph, "Compassion" mention — optional single still from the field-photography era if a usable shot exists in the archive; otherwise stay on Dan.
  - Identity paragraph, "Borne" mention — optional Borne brand mark or a shirt-on-rack still; otherwise stay on Dan.
  - Activity paragraph, "creative products and workflows" line — optional cut to a real side-project screen (Lia design file, NOBONK packaging, Voxquote app, robson.studio recording, Claude window). Don't name them on camera. The visual hints at the work without making the video about specific products.
  - Hook + crossroads + close — talking head, plain. No B-roll.
- Captions on every spoken line, same font + position as V1.
- No music. Voice carries.
- Close lands plain. No music swell, no end-card flourish, no logo. Say it and stop.
- Read the script out loud before shooting. Cut whatever feels forced — Dan to trim 1-2 sentences on camera if pacing demands.

## Material saved for future videos (deliberately not in this script)

The earlier longer iterations included material that's strong but didn't fit the snappy manifesto shape. Save for dedicated videos:

- **Borne Clothing exit** — its own video about lessons from building and selling a small clothing company.
- **Humanitarian photography arc** (Compassion field photography) — its own video, already slotted V8-12 per [[02-bench]].
- **Client work** (King Living, Sweat, Compassion, Prezzee) — specific design-process videos (logo brainstorm, brand decisions, system trade-offs) drawing from real client work.
- **Named side projects** (Lia, NOBONK, Voxquote, OpenScripture) — each gets its own Build / Frame video as appropriate. The intro deliberately doesn't name them so each one gets to land on its own video without the audience already having a frame for it.
- **The bet sentence** ("a company that earns while I'm doing bedtime") — strong stake but cut from this version to keep the manifesto shape. Belongs in a Founder-pillar video about the actual bet structure.
- **The AI-as-unlock framing** ("Claude in the loop, the unlock I've been waiting for") — belongs in an AI / tooling video about the specific workflow change.

## Caption (TikTok post)

Draft:

> Lead UX designer + dad + side-building a company that earns when I'm not in a meeting. Posting the work so I keep going.

## Hashtags (low priority, lock once)

`#ux` `#uxdesign` `#designsystems` `#aiindesign` `#dadlife` `#newcastle`

4-6 max.

---

## Open variables — resolved 2026-05-19

1. **Replace V3 in the seed batch, or run as V3-alt alongside the original chronological-CV V3?** Resolved: replace (Dan-confirmed). Original chronological-CV V3 stays preserved at [[03-how-i-got-here]] for the iteration record, but this version is the canonical V3 for the seed batch.
2. **B-roll decision**: Resolved — Dan to figure out at shoot. Script doesn't depend on B-roll to land; can ship as pure talking head if B-roll prep adds friction.
3. **Visual signature**: Resolved — re-use V1's signature. Dan-confirmed: "yeah, it's fine. Let's leave that as is. I don't need to be worried about that at this level."
4. **Compassion B-roll**: Resolved — same as #2, Dan to figure out at shoot.

## Decisions logged (no longer open)

- **Script content locked 2026-05-19.** Dan-confirmed: "This is my tone of voice. I'm really happy with this."
- **Viewer-question hook** ("How do you build something new without losing time with your family?") over identity-first opener. Frames Dan as a peer asking the same question; earns the "watch me figure this out" close.
- **Flowing prose over staccato chip-stack.** Script reads as Dan introducing himself at a dinner party. Conversational pace, ~65-75s.
- **Compassion named** in the identity paragraph. Humanitarian-photography arc is now a single specific credential, not vague past work.
- **Two friends NOT named.** Solo framing intentional. Team mention reserved for Build-pillar videos where the cofounders are part of the content.
- **No bet sentence** ("company that earns while doing bedtime") — saved for a Founder-pillar video. The "family having the best of me" line carries the emotional stake instead.
- **No client names spoken on camera.** Reserved for design-process videos.
- **No individual product names** (Lia / NOBONK / Voxquote / OpenScripture). Reserved for individual Build / Frame videos.
- **No timeframe delta** in the hook. The viewer-question pattern doesn't need one.
- **Vulnerability close** ("never really made TikToks before, follow along") — earned honesty, not engagement-bait. On-voice for warm-up scale.
- **No em dashes anywhere.** Voice rule compliance.
- **"I feel like" used 2x intentionally** — once to frame the crossroads (felt state), once to name the FOMO (the actual feeling). Middle use dropped per voice rule.
- **"Moving so fast"** kept — Dan-confirmed as how he actually talks. The "so" intensifier survives the voice rule because it's tone-true.

## Source material

- [[tiktok-intro-script-patterns]] (research + 4 rewrite options memo)
- [[03-how-i-got-here]] (preserved chronological-CV original)
- [[01-decision-paralysis]] (working comparison — close matches the bet shape)
- [[tiktok-voice-hooks]] (voice rules informing every beat)
- robson.studio (client logo source: Prezzee, NobleOak, Grays, King Living, Scenic, money.com.au, Compassion, Aurizon)
- Dan-confirmed: Borne Clothing built and sold ~2025

## Pre-shoot checklist

- [ ] Source Borne Clothing brand mark + a shirt-on-rack still
- [ ] Source clean logo versions of King Living, Sweat, Compassion, plus optional Prezzee / NobleOak / money.com.au
- [ ] Capture Lia / NOBONK / Voxquote / OpenScripture B-roll (real WIP, not mockups)
- [ ] Capture Claude / Cursor screen recording (representative current project)
- [ ] Lock the visual signature (use V1's shirt + chair + window)
- [ ] Confirm caption + hashtags
- [ ] Decide: replace V3 in seed batch, or ship as V3-alt
- [ ] Confirm hook delivery on camera before committing to take

## Related

- [[02-bench]] (Compassion field photography slotted V8-12 as its own video)

- [[tiktok-voice-hooks]]
- [[tiktok-intro-script-patterns]] (the research that drove this rewrite)

drafts.title: V1 — The Take, Decision Paralysis (terrible at being my own client)
drafts.hook: I'm genuinely terrible at being my own client.
drafts.close: Posting it here keeps me honest. I'm Dan, designer in Newcastle. Most weeks I'll post a piece of work, or something from the life behind it.
drafts.caption: Most designers I know have the same problem. We build well for clients and badly for ourselves. Studio site sat in Figma for two years. Loupe sat in my head for years. AI tooling has changed the equation. Posting the work here so I keep going.
drafts.body_markdown:
# V1 — The Take: Decision Paralysis

First video on the page. Personal admission opening, forward direction underneath. Substitutes for the original "Thesis Take" V1 in [[tiktok-plan]] per [[03-queue]].

## Why this video opens the page

It opens with a vulnerable line that lots of designers and founders will recognise. No portfolio language, no thesis monologue. The work shows up as evidence inside the admission, not as the lead. Pulls from material that has already landed on LinkedIn (Robson Studio launch post, Loupe announcement, the "creative courage" draft).

Trade-off accepted: doesn't establish the page's full scope on day one. That's fine, V2 (texture) and V3 (arc) round out the spine in week one.

---

## Script

**Hook (0-3s)** — talking head, chest-up, soft window light, plain:
> "I'm genuinely terrible at being my own client."

**Beat 1 (3-15s)** — talking head:
> "I build fast and high quality for paid work. For my own ideas, decision paralysis wins. Most designers I know have the same problem."

**Beat 2 (15-35s)** — cut to B-roll: robson.studio site, Loupe app on phone, Figma file timestamps showing months of dust:
> "My studio site sat in Figma for two years. I built the live version in three sessions with Claude. An app I'd wanted to make for years took less than a day. Both were waiting on me, not on the tools."

**Beat 3 (35-55s)** — back to talking head:
> "What changed is I started running my own stuff like client work. Brief. Build context. Set rules. Ship rough. Refine. Same loop I run for paid work, applied to me. With AI tooling the loop is fast enough that I actually finish things now."

**Close (55-65s)** — talking head, plain:
> "Posting it here keeps me honest. I'm Dan, designer in Newcastle. Most weeks I'll post a piece of work, or something from the life behind it."

**Word count**: ~143 words. Target 60-65s at TikTok pace.

---

## Production notes

- Talking head bookends. Lock the visual signature on this video. Same shirt, same chair, same window for every Take going forward.
- B-roll for beat 2:
  - Screen recording of robson.studio (scroll the page, hover the cursor grid, the cycling hero word)
  - Screen recording or phone capture of Loupe (open the app, the lens screen)
  - Optional: Figma file thumbnail with the "edited 2 years ago" timestamp visible
- Cuts tight. No shot longer than 3-4s on a static frame.
- Captions on every line, same font and position you'll use forward.
- No music. Or very low ambient.
- Word "genuinely" in the hook lands as a Dan-voice marker (it's how you actually open the LinkedIn version of this). Keep it.

## Caption (TikTok post)

Draft:
> Most designers I know have the same problem. We build well for clients and badly for ourselves. Studio site sat in Figma for two years. Loupe sat in my head for years. AI tooling has changed the equation. Posting the work here so I keep going.

## Hashtags (low priority, lock once)

`#ux` `#uxdesign` `#designsystems` `#aiindesign` `#decisionparalysis` `#newcastle`

4-6 max. Don't over-tag.

---

## Source material this draws from

- LinkedIn: Robson Studio launch post (1mo ago, 797 impressions) — "Every designer I know has some version of this problem. You build brilliantly for clients and then stall completely on your own stuff (decision paralysis is real)."
- LinkedIn: Loupe announcement (2mo ago, 608 impressions) — "I have always found it hard to prioritise my own passion projects, but with agentic tooling that equation has changed completely."
- Vault draft: [[08-creative-courage]] — "I'm genuinely terrible at being my own client."
- [[personal-brand]]
- [[lia]]

## Pre-shoot checklist

- [ ] Pick the shirt for the talking head (lock for all future Takes/Builds)
- [ ] Decide the chair + window angle (slight low angle, soft light)
- [ ] Test screen recording for robson.studio (1080x1920 vertical or square crop)
- [ ] Capture Loupe screen flow (or take a clean phone-in-hand shot)
- [ ] Confirm caption + hashtags
- [ ] Confirm handle and bio-line are live before scheduling

drafts.title: Lia Offsite — Day 1 (draft)
drafts.hook: Chris, Luke and I have set aside three days to align on what this year could look like as we look to start a business together. Excited and nervous all at once. Day 1.
drafts.close: Day 2 is for [what tomorrow is for]. More tomorrow.
drafts.body_markdown:
# Lia Offsite — Day 1 (draft)

For the three-day cofounder getaway with [[chris-quinton]] and [[luke-odea]]. Posted as the first of a four-part arc (intro/setup → Day 1 → Day 2 → Day 3) or as a standalone hybrid post that carries its own intro.

Format: **Texture-Build hybrid**. The visuals do the show-not-tell work (cofounder offsite is inherently filmic), the voiceover lands one real moment from the day.

Lia thesis (musician admin problem, modular tools, etc.) is held back from the intro on purpose. Personal stakes lead. The business context can reveal over Day 2 and Day 3.

---

## Script

**Intro (0-12s)** — talking head, locked visual signature (same shirt/chair/window as V1), OR shot at the location on arrival:
> "Chris, Luke and I have set aside three days to align on what this year could look like as we look to start a business together. Excited and nervous all at once. Day 1."

**Beat 1 (12-25s)** — montage: arrival, the space, Chris and Luke, gear unpacked, whiteboards, food, the view:
> "Today we mapped the year. Who does what. When we actually start. What the first thing we make is."

**Beat 2 (25-40s)** — quieter shot, one specific image:
> "[A real moment from the day. A line one of you said. A whiteboard photo. The view at the end of it. Fill this in after Day 1.]"

**Close (40-50s)** — light fading, or three of you in frame:
> "Day 2 is for [what tomorrow is for]. More tomorrow."

**Word count**: ~85 words. ~45-50s.

---

## Production notes

Capture throughout the day, not at the end:

- Ricoh GR IV stills: arrival, the space, food, hands, whiteboards, walks, light at different times of day, the three of you. Confirm camera + posting consent with CQ and Luke before Day 1 starts.
- Phone video: 2-3s of motion per moment, not minutes. Easier to edit, harder to ramble.
- Whiteboard photos at the end of each session. They double as B-roll and as session notes for the shared Lia vault.
- One quiet shot at the end of Day 1 (light, view, empty room). Hold it 3-4s. That becomes the close.

Edit constraints:

- No music or very low ambient.
- 1-2s per shot mostly. Land the close on something held longer.
- Captions on every spoken line, same font/position as V1.
- Same visual signature for the intro talking head as V1 (locks the page identity across content).

---

## Voice direction (iterated from session 2026-05-10)

Initial draft led with the musician-admin problem ("Most independent musicians spend more time on admin than on the actual music..."). Dan pushed back: too thesis-y, too explainer-y for an intro. Resolved by leading with the human stakes ("Chris, Luke and I have set aside three days..."), and pulling the business specifics down a layer ("Who does what. When we actually start. What the first thing we make is.").

Trade-off accepted: viewers don't learn what Lia is in Day 1. That tension carries them into Day 2 and Day 3.

---

## Alternative structure (Option B: standalone setup video)

If filming + posting before the trip starts:

- Post a 20-30s "setup" video using just the Intro segment expanded slightly. Day 1, 2, 3 then ride on it as tight ~30-45s Texture posts without needing to re-explain context.
- Reads as a four-part arc rather than three disconnected daily updates.
- Only viable if there's time before the trip kicks off.

---

## Day 2 and Day 3 stubs (fill in during/after the trip)

### Day 2

**Hook (0-3s)**: [Day 2 frame. What today is for.]

**Beat 1 (3-20s)**: [The hardest decision of the day. The argument worth naming.]

**Beat 2 (20-35s)**: [Specific moment — a line, a whiteboard, a view.]

**Close (35-45s)**: [What Day 3 is for.]

### Day 3

**Hook (0-3s)**: [Day 3 frame. Last day.]

**Beat 1 (3-20s)**: [What we're leaving with. The decisions that stuck.]

**Beat 2 (20-35s)**: [Final moment — driving home shot, last whiteboard, a quiet view.]

**Close (35-45s)**: [The plain statement. What happens next. Or just: "Done. Going home."]

---
